svg.text: MODERN HEBREW
HISTORY — REVIVAL AND COEXISTENCE
THE HEBREW REVIVAL
1881: Ben-Yehuda arrives in Palestine; speaks only Hebrew to son Ben-Zion
First Hebrew-speaking child in modern history (Ben-Zion, b. 1882)
First Hebrew newspaper: HaZvi (1884) • first Hebrew dictionary: 1910-1959
1948: Hebrew declared official language of Israel; 600,000 speakers
INGATHERING OF EXILES AND LANGUAGES
Waves of immigration: Yiddish, Arabic, Russian, Amharic, Ladino speakers
All learned Hebrew; Hebrew absorbed words from every immigrant language
Mizrahi Jews from Arab countries: ~40% of Jewish population by 1960s
They brought Arabic phonology, vocabulary, and music into Hebrew culture
MODERN HEBREW TODAY
~9M speakers; mother tongue for majority of Israeli Jews
Only example of dead language fully revived as a living language
Growing diaspora communities learning Hebrew (Jewish and non-Jewish)
ARABIC IN MODERN ISRAEL · SECONDARY LANGUAGE
ARAB CITIZENS OF ISRAEL
~2M Arab citizens of Israel: Muslim, Christian, Druze communities
Predominantly speak Levantine Arabic + Modern Hebrew as second language
Arabic-medium schools exist; Arabic media, literature, universities
THE 1948 NAKBA AND ITS LINGUISTIC LEGACY
~700,000 Palestinian Arabs displaced in 1948 war (Nakba = "catastrophe")
Palestinian Arabic dialects now spoken in Jordan, Lebanon, Gaza, West Bank
Arabic place names: many Israeli cities retain Arabic-origin names
Haifa = Arabic Hayfa • Jaffa = Arabic Yafa • Beer Sheva = Arabic
TWO SEMITIC LANGUAGES, ONE LAND
Hebrew and Arabic: Semitic siblings, same script family, same root system
Political separation does not undo linguistic kinship
Shalom / Salaam: same root, same meaning, same aspiration
Future generations will inherit both languages: one land's two voices
Remember: these words came from the same mouth, before they were two
PLATE 12d ALT · MODERN HEBREW / ARABIC · HISTORY
